title: Nebula Courier Co.: Mastering Timeouts and Cancellation in asyncio
details: Learner is beginner-to-intermediate with asyncio: comfortable with async def, await, coroutines, futures, and asyncio.gather. NOT yet exposed to queues or synchronization primitives (locks, events, semaphores) — avoid these entirely. Learning goal: master asyncio.wait_for (timeouts) and Task.cancel() (manual cancellation), including handling asyncio.TimeoutError and asyncio.CancelledError. Narrative: learner plays dispatcher at 'Nebula Courier Co.', a company that sends autonomous delivery drones across star systems. Drones are simulated via async functions that 'travel' using asyncio.sleep. Space is unpredictable: some drones take too long (need timeouts via wait_for) and some get stuck entirely due to wormhole interference (need manual abort via Task.cancel()). Problem set builds progressively: single-coroutine timeout handling -> manual task cancellation with cleanup -> combining both across concurrent tasks with gather. Tone: friendly, approachable, lightly humorous space-courier flavor. Keep scope narrow — do not introduce queues, locks, or other sync primitives; only wait_for, Task.cancel(), CancelledError, TimeoutError, create_task, and gather (already known).

parts.title: Welcome to Nebula Courier Co.
parts.details: Introduction/story-setup part only. Establishes the learner as a new dispatcher at Nebula Courier Co., a scrappy interstellar delivery company. Explains that drones are async coroutines simulating travel via asyncio.sleep. Foreshadows two recurring problems: (1) clients who won't wait forever for a delivery (timeouts), and (2) drones that vanish into wormholes and never come back (need manual cancellation). Sets tone: friendly, a little chaotic, space-courier humor. Previews that the learner will build tools to handle both situations, then combine them into a full fleet dashboard.
parts.description_agent_notes:
- Write welcoming narrative intro, no exercises here
- Establish setting: Nebula Courier Co, drones = coroutines, travel time = asyncio.sleep duration
- Introduce two recurring problems in friendly tone: impatient clients (timeouts) and lost drones (stuck coroutines needing cancellation)
- Reference their existing skills (async def, await, coroutines, gather) as the foundation they're building on
- Preview the 3 upcoming missions/parts without giving away implementation details
- Keep tone light, approachable, a bit of space humor okay
- No code or technical instructions needed here

parts.title: The Impatient Client
parts.details: First hands-on part: introduce asyncio.wait_for. Learner writes/completes a coroutine deliver_package(package_id, travel_time) that simulates drone travel via asyncio.sleep(travel_time) then returns a success message/dict. Then writes attempt_delivery_with_timeout(package_id, travel_time, timeout) which wraps deliver_package in asyncio.wait_for(timeout=...), catches asyncio.TimeoutError, and returns a clear status (e.g. 'delivered' vs 'timed out'). Should test with multiple travel_time/timeout combos to see both outcomes.
parts.description_agent_notes:
- Explain asyncio.wait_for(coro, timeout) mechanics: races the coroutine against a timer
- Explain that wait_for raises asyncio.TimeoutError if coroutine doesn't finish in time, and cancels the underlying task automatically
- Frame as: impatient clients only wait X seconds for their package before giving up
- Use concrete story numbers (e.g. client waits 3 seconds, drone takes 5 seconds -> timeout)
- Note: this builds directly on their known await/async def skills, wait_for is just a new wrapper
- Do NOT mention queues or locks
- Keep it focused on single-task timeout handling (no concurrency yet)
parts.coding_agent_notes:
- Provide skeleton: async def deliver_package(package_id, travel_time) -> simulate with await asyncio.sleep(travel_time); return dict/string with package_id and 'delivered'
- Provide skeleton: async def attempt_delivery_with_timeout(package_id, travel_time, timeout) -- learner fills in body using asyncio.wait_for and try/except asyncio.TimeoutError
- On timeout, return a status like {'package_id':id,'status':'timed_out'}
- Include a small runner (main coroutine) that calls attempt_delivery_with_timeout with at least 2 scenarios: one where delivery succeeds within timeout, one where it times out
- Use asyncio.run(main()) pattern learner is already familiar with
- Keep imports minimal: only asyncio
- No manual task creation or cancel() yet -- that's next part

parts.title: Runaway Drone
parts.details: Second hands-on part: introduce Task.cancel() and asyncio.CancelledError. Learner writes a coroutine simulating a drone that gets stuck in a wormhole (e.g. an infinite loop with periodic sleeps and print/log 'still searching...', or a very long sleep) wrapped in try/except asyncio.CancelledError to perform cleanup (print 'aborting mission, returning to base') before re-raising. Learner then creates this coroutine as a Task via asyncio.create_task, waits/monitors briefly (e.g. await asyncio.sleep(short_time)), then calls task.cancel() manually, and awaits the task wrapped in try/except CancelledError to confirm cancellation, checking task.cancelled().
parts.description_agent_notes:
- Introduce concept: some drones don't just take too long, they get truly stuck (simulating infinite/very long operation) and timeouts alone won't fit the narrative -- mission control must actively abort
- Explain asyncio.create_task creates a Task object which can be cancelled directly via .cancel()
- Explain that .cancel() causes a CancelledError to be raised inside the coroutine at its next await point
- Emphasize that a coroutine can catch CancelledError to do cleanup, but should typically re-raise it (or let it propagate) so the task is correctly marked cancelled
- Explain task.cancelled() as a way to confirm cancellation happened
- Story: drone enters a wormhole and starts 'searching for way out' in a loop; after mission control waits a bit impatiently, they hit the abort button
- Keep focused only on single-task manual cancellation, not combined with wait_for yet
parts.coding_agent_notes:
- Provide skeleton: async def lost_drone(drone_id) -- loop with try/except asyncio.CancelledError: inside try, loop with await asyncio.sleep(1) and print 'drone {id} still searching...'; inside except, print cleanup message then re-raise
- Provide skeleton: async def abort_mission(drone_id) -- learner fills in: create task via asyncio.create_task(lost_drone(drone_id)), await asyncio.sleep(small fixed time e.g. 3), call task.cancel(), then await the task inside try/except asyncio.CancelledError, then print/return whether task.cancelled() is True
- Provide a main() runner calling abort_mission and using asyncio.run
- Keep imports minimal: only asyncio
- Do not introduce wait_for combination yet -- that's the capstone part
- Comments in starter code should hint where to call .cancel() and where to catch CancelledError

parts.title: Fleet Coordination
parts.details: Capstone part combining everything: learner builds a mini fleet dispatch system managing several drones concurrently using asyncio.gather (already known) or a list of tasks. Some drones are dispatched with asyncio.wait_for timeouts (from Part 2 logic), others are monitored and manually cancelled if they exceed a 'patience threshold' using Task.cancel() (from Part 3 logic). Learner aggregates final report showing which drones delivered successfully, which timed out, and which were manually cancelled, echoing the mission-control dashboard theme.
parts.description_agent_notes:
- Frame as final challenge: dispatcher must manage a full fleet at once, not just one drone
- Recap briefly (1-2 lines) that they'll reuse both wait_for (for drones with a fixed patience limit) and manual create_task+cancel (for drones being actively monitored)
- Explain that some drones in the fleet will be wrapped in wait_for calls (auto-timeout), others will be created as raw Tasks that get cancelled manually mid-flight based on a simple condition (e.g. elapsed time check)
- Explain final aggregation: build a report/list summarizing each drone's outcome: 'delivered', 'timed_out', or 'cancelled'
- Keep story tone playful: 'End of shift report for Nebula Courier Co.'
- Reiterate no queues/locks needed -- gather + wait_for + cancel is sufficient
- This ties together all previous parts into one coherent program
parts.coding_agent_notes:
- Reuse/adapt deliver_package and lost_drone-style coroutines from Parts 2 and 3 (redefine simplified versions locally if needed for self-containment)
- Provide a list of drone configs, e.g. list of dicts with package_id, travel_time, mode ('timeout' or 'manual'), and timeout/patience values
- Provide skeleton: async def dispatch_fleet(drone_configs) -- learner fills in logic:
  - for 'timeout' mode drones: wrap delivery coroutine in asyncio.wait_for, catch TimeoutError
  - for 'manual' mode drones: create task via asyncio.create_task, await asyncio.sleep(patience), then task.cancel(), catch CancelledError
  - collect all outcomes into a results list/dict per drone_id with status field
- Use asyncio.gather(*coroutines_or_tasks, return_exceptions=True) or manage manually as learner prefers, but starter code should scaffold a clear structure combining both handling paths
- Provide a main() that builds drone_configs (mix of both modes, some succeeding, some timing out, some being cancelled) and prints a final formatted 'End of Shift Report'
- Keep imports minimal: only asyncio
- Include inline comments marking where wait_for logic goes vs where manual cancel logic goes to reinforce the two techniques learned
